content.problemStatement:
Stack has NNN distinct cards, numbered 111 to NNN. He wonders how many pairs of arrays (A,B)(A,B)(A,B) are there such that:


∣A∣=∣B∣=N|A|=|B|=N∣A∣=∣B∣=N;
1≤Ai,Bi≤N1 \leq A_i, B_i \leq N1≤Ai​,Bi​≤N;
Ai≠BiA_i \neq B_iAi​=Bi​;
If he writes AiA_iAi​ on the upper face of ithi^{th}ith card and BiB_iBi​ on the lower face of ithi^{th}ith card, it is possible to flip some of the cards in such a way that each integer from 111 to NNN is present on the upper face of **exactly one** card.
Since number of pairs might be huge, please print it modulo MMM.
It is guaranteed that MMM is prime.


Input Format

The only line of input contains two positive integers NNN and MMM, number of cards and the required modulo.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the number of pairs (A,B)(A,B)(A,B) satisfying the conditions modulo MMM.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The does not exist any pair of arrays (A,B)(A,B)(A,B) which satisfy the given conditions.
